original_abstract: Universities are increasingly turning to third-party agents and recruiters to bolster their international student enrollment. This article examines the core criticisms and systemic challenges associated with this practice, highlighting the ethical and operational risks involved. By analyzing current industry trends, the study offers strategic recommendations for universities and policymakers to align their recruitment practices with the best interests of both the institutions and the prospective students they serve.
humanized_abstract: The recruitment of third party agents and agents or recruitment consultants by Universities to aid in their international student recruitment activities has become a common and increasingly prevalent practice in recent years. This paper outlines and examines the criticisms, and the underlying issues of this practice, including its ethical and practical implications. Through an examination of the current state of the sector, this paper makes several recommendations for the practice of agent recruitment in the context of international student recruitment; recommendations that are intended to ensure that the practices and priorities of universities and governments are aligned with the need to protect the welfare of students and to maintain quality international education opportunities.
